protocol: medical - supraventricular tachycardia (including atrial fibrillation) medical - tachycardia medical - ventricular tachycardia with a pulse (protocol 2 - 8)

dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 1 responding for breathing problems at 78-year-old male in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I can't catch my breath. It just started happening all of a sudden.
No chest pain now, but earlier he said he had some.
Mr. Charles, can you tell me your name?
My name is Charles.
Mr. Charles, can you tell me the city we're in?
I'm in Richmond, for sure.
What day is it today?
Saturday, I think. Maybe Sunday. Not sure.
Can you explain why you called 911 today?
I couldn't breathe. I woke up and it felt like my chest and lungs weren't working.
Open your eyes and follow my finger with your gaze if you can.
I can do that easily. It's clear too.
What is your birthday?
April 3rd, 1941. Still got it in the head.
Squeeze my finger if you understand me, please.
There you go. Solid grip and all.
I'll look into your airway. Can you open wide with your tongue out?
Sure, take a good look if you will.
Airway is clear. You're breathing on your own. Anything in your throat recently?
No. Clean and clear, nothing sticking out or lodged in there.
Take a deep breath now so I can listen to your lungs a bit.
Hmmm, okay here it goes.
You're breathing fast and your chest sounds a bit wet. It's likely labored breathing.
Counting respirations. It's 28 per minute right now.
Any chest discomfort while taking those breaths earlier today?
A little pain but mostly gone by now.
That's a sign of distress. We'll give some more support for breathing shortly.
Pulse is 138 and strong. Measuring right now.
Blood pressure is 158 over 98. Too high for comfort, still working though.
SpO2 is at 81 percent. Needs improvement for breathable air in the lungs at the moment.

Tell me more about your breathing. Did it come suddenly or slowly with time today? 34. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.:signs_and_symptoms; Patient: Suddenly. It just began and I felt like I was suffocating upon waking up.
Was it caused by something at the table or activity?
I was just sleeping. I hadn't eaten or moved actively, not even a sip since water.

Do you have any known allergies to food or drugs currently that I need to know about to proceed properly? 38. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.:allergies; Patient: No known drug or food allergies. No reactions that have ever been documented thus far in my life. 39. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.:medications; EMT: What medications are you currently taking for issues or blood pressure, for example? 40. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.:medications; Patient: Lasix, Plavix, Albuterol, Crestor, Cozaar, Cordarone, and Aldactazide. My team set the doses, and I'm staying on track.
What is your medical history that helps us assess at this moment?
I had a heart attack before, and I manage hypertension, Asthma, Heart Failure, CVA, pulmonary hypertension, and fluid stuff here with my provider.
Tell me more exactly when you first noticed these symptoms on occasion in time to your routine.
I was just in bed at 10 PM when I woke up gripping at my chest. It all happened in an instant.
Was this your first time being like that in your health?
No. I had a similar event a week ago that brought me to the hospital then. This one feels similar but different from that one.
When did the chest sensation and breathing difficulty first begin?
At the start of my difficulty breathing, it came in like maybe three minutes prior to waking up at 10 PM.
Did anything make the sensation or breathing better or worse?

I don't exactly know what did, but sitting upright felt better for the lungs in that case. 51. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.:quality; Paramedic: How would you describe that chest sensation when it was still bothering you? 52. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.:quality; Patient: Like heavy pressure. I felt like I had to struggle to get a deep breath.

Does the discomfort move to other parts of the body like the neck or jaw? 54. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.:radiation; Patient: No movement. It was in the middle of my sternum and didn't go anywhere else to feel the rest. 55. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.:severity; Paramedic: On a scale from 1 to 10, how intense was the discomfort?
I'd put it at five. Not the worst but definitely uncomfortable, like a mirror of that recent episode.

Did the discomfort go away after a while or subside long enough to be forgotten again? 58. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.:time; Patient: It lasted about ten minutes and then stopped. I haven't had it since.
Checking your skin now. Any rashes, bumps, coldness, swelling, or discoloration?
My skin still feels warm and dry to the touch so far as I feel it undisturbed.
I'll use light to examine your eyes and pupils. Confirmed, please.
Confirmed. Go ahead and check for anything we're unaware of here.
Pupils are round and equal. Now closely follow the light while I move it left and right.
Like this? Compensated ease for you, if it's not perfect, I know it is.
Let me quickly check your throat and airway. Breathe normally.
All clear in those areas. No abnormalities there that I can feel or observe.
I'll listen to your lungs closely. Deep breaths like the first time, please.
Your breath sounds are wet in the back during inspiration.
You have abnormal lung sounds. Can you lie back for more listening?
Feel free to examine me like a textbook.
Now, I'll assess your neck and look at your airway anatomy.
Your neck veins appear flat, and you have no signs of trauma or stiffness.
Initial 4-lead ECG. This will start monitoring your heart rhythm.
Performing a 4-lead ECG to monitor for any arrhythmia.
Performing 12-lead ECG to get a full assessment of your heart's function.
12 Lead ECG complete. Findings include ST elevation in II, AVF, III, and anterior and posterior.
EKG findings also show right ventricular strain.
No lymph nodes or masses in your neck. No trauma to the area.
We also noted inferior ST-elevation and potential anterior ischemia.
Does your stomach hurt or feel hard?
No, I'm fine with my food.
Your GCS is 15, and you respond to questions appropriately.
There is no edema on initial impression on the extremities or other areas.
HEENT is atraumatic. Facial features are symmetrical. No drainage or swelling.
Move your legs up and down so I can check sensation and strength.
They feel right as rain with no pain or swelling on either side.
Pulse is now at 104 compared to when we started.
Respirations have increased slightly to 29 as minutes go by.
Blood pressure reading down to 132 over 89, still not low enough but more within acceptable bounds.
Glucose was 110 earlier in the first measurement before clarification.
SpO2 has now climbed to 95 percent with support in place.
Ronan, this EKG shows sinus tach and ST elevation.
We're applying a CPAP to assist your breathing right now.
CPAP mask is on and running smoothly.
Administering high-flow oxygen continuously based on our protocol.
Starting an IV in the left hand via an extremity vein.
Given the CPAP, vital signs, EKG findings, and IV established, I'd say we're following STEMI, pulmonary distress, and tachycardia protocols.
Pulse drops to 104. Will reassess every 15 minutes as protocol goes.
Respirations still at 29 expect them to be okay with active CPAP support.
Blood pressure is at 132 over 89, still with the trend of downward movement.
SpO2 level holds at 95 with EKG and oxygen monitoring active.
Is the breathing still difficult after wearing the supportive mask over the time now?
A bit, not like before in description details.
Take a deep breath again. Does the CPAP help your breathing capacity while we monitor changes?
Yes, the mask does help, reliably make more air in and out than before.
Any new changes like dizziness or chest heaviness show up in time between now and then?
No more change than the occasional tightness in the central area, otherwise just tired from breathing deeply more than usual.
No current pain at this time based on patient report.
Given your condition, we will transport you to VCU Medical Center now.
We will follow protocol and transport to VCU as directed.
We are preparing for transport to VCU Medical Center right now.
Placing you on a stretcher while supporting CPAP and oxygen during the travel.
Tell us when you are ready for that movement from wherever you are.
I'm ready. Let's do this now.
Securely onto the stretcher with a calm and easy placement.
CPAP is in place and functioning well. We'll monitor distress for the duration of the trip.
We will take you promptly to the ED at VCU and ensure a complete handover with all items detailed for the medical team.
I feel a bit more at ease now.
Oxygen is on with continuous monitoring active.
We are on our way now. Safe hands at VCU for a full assessment at handover.
VCU Medical Center acknowledged. Prepare for arrival and clear zones.
